text: Upon introduction of the camera, the small bowel, terminal ileum, cecum, and ascending colon are visible. The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the appendix.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the mesoappendix, providing a sharp incision and effective hemostasis. The appendix and mesoappendix are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers downward towards the base of the appendix, ensuring not to injure the adjacent cecum.

The surgeon reaches the base of the appendix and uses a knot pusher with chromic catgut to ligate the base at the roder's node. The appendix is held and pushed upwards as the knot is tightened, completing the application of the proximal knot.
The surgeon applies the distal knot to the appendix, causing a visible change in color due to devascularization.
The surgeon cuts the appendix between the two applied knots, using suction to remove any spillage of bowel contents.
The surgeon checks for free fluid in the pouch of Douglas below the uterus, visibly identifying and suctioning out the fluid. The appendix is then removed through the umbilical port.
The surgeon performs a bowel walk to inspect for any abnormalities in Meckel's diverticulum, carefully examining the intestinal tract for signs of diverticulum presence or other anomalies.
The surgeon presents the retrieved appendix specimen, which appears devascularized and discolored.
